title: data() getter clones the entire pixel buffer on every JS call, doubling peak heap under WASM memory limits
description: The #[wasm_bindgen(getter)] for `data` does `self.data.clone()`, allocating a full copy of the decoded RGBA buffer each time JS reads the property. For a full-resolution JPEG this can be several MB. Because WASM memory never shrinks, repeated reads (e.g. in a loop or accidental double-access) cause the WASM heap to grow monotonically. The correct pattern is to expose a zero-copy view via `js_sys::Uint8Array::view` or to move ownership out once with an `into_data() -> Vec<u8>` method that consumes self.
evidence: Line 27 literally calls `self.data.clone()` — confirmed in source. The `packages/fast-jpeg/src/index.ts` wrapper reads `.data` once and calls `r.free()`, but the public API offers no protection against multiple reads; wasm-bindgen also copies the returned Vec into a Uint8Array, so each read produces two full-size allocations. WASM heap never shrinks, making each extra read a permanent cost.

title: decode_scaled has no cancellation path; long decodes on the WASM thread block the entire JS event loop
description: WASM executes synchronously on the JS thread. A large JPEG passed to decode_scaled will block the browser main thread (or worker) for the entire decode duration with no way for the caller to cancel or time-box the operation. Because jpeg_decoder processes the image in a single synchronous call, there is no natural yield point. The opportunity is to move decode_scaled calls into a dedicated Web Worker (already present in the architecture) so the main thread is never blocked, or to document that callers must always invoke this from a worker context.
evidence: The architecture already routes all JPEG decode through workers (jxl-worker-browser / jxl-worker-node layers). The `packages/fast-jpeg/src/index.ts` wrapper is Node-only (uses `createRequire`). Call sites in `timings/fastest/` are benchmark scripts, not browser main-thread code. Blocking is a real property of synchronous WASM but the constraint is already satisfied by caller convention — this is a documentation gap at most, not a code defect.

title: DecodeResult::data getter clones the entire pixel buffer on every JS read
description: The `data` getter (line 27) calls `self.data.clone()`, allocating a new Vec<u8> of full decoded pixel size on every call from JavaScript. A 12MP image at RGBA8 = 48 MB cloned per getter access. wasm-bindgen will also copy the Vec into a Uint8Array on the JS side, so a single call produces two full-size allocations. The fix is to return a `js_sys::Uint8Array` view via `unsafe { js_sys::Uint8Array::view(&self.data) }` (zero-copy if the WASM memory isn't grown) or expose a `data_ptr`/`data_len` pair and let JS wrap it.
evidence: Same clone at line 27, confirmed in source — duplicate finding of concurrency-1 on the same defect. wasm-bindgen converts `Vec<u8>` return to a JS `Uint8Array` by copying into WASM-heap-allocated memory and then into JS; this means every `.data` access costs two full pixel-buffer allocations. A `js_sys::Uint8Array::view` would eliminate both copies.

title: to_rgba RGB24 path builds the output buffer with individual push calls per pixel, causing repeated small writes
description: The RGB24 branch (lines 84-87) calls `rgba.extend_from_slice(chunk)` + `rgba.push(255)` per 3-byte pixel. While `Vec::with_capacity` avoids reallocation, the inner loop issues two separate write operations per pixel. For a typical 12MP image this is ~12M iterations with ~24M Vec write calls. An alternative is to write 4 bytes at once via an unrolled `[chunk[0], chunk[1], chunk[2], 255]` extend, or use a SIMD-friendly interleave. This is a moderate gain (5-15%) but meaningful given this is the hot path for every JPEG decode.
evidence: With `opt-level=3`, `lto=fat`, `codegen-units=1` (all set in Cargo.toml profile.release), Rust's LLVM backend fuses `extend_from_slice` on a 3-byte slice + `push(255)` into a single 4-byte store and auto-vectorizes the `chunks_exact(3)` loop. The finder's claim of '24M Vec write calls' describes the source-level abstraction, not the emitted instructions. No concrete performance regression exists at the code level.

title: to_rgba L8 grayscale path calls extend_from_slice with a temporary 4-byte array allocation per pixel
description: The L8 branch (line 93) calls `rgba.extend_from_slice(&[lum, lum, lum, 255])` which constructs a stack-allocated `[u8; 4]` per iteration. Although the slice is stack-local, the loop still performs `pixels.len()` iterations of 4-byte writes via the slice indirection rather than writing a single u32 directly. Using `rgba.extend_from_slice` on a `u32::to_ne_bytes` or writing four bytes via `unsafe` pointer arithmetic would eliminate the per-pixel overhead.
evidence: At opt-level=3, `&[lum, lum, lum, 255]` is a stack-local `[u8;4]` that LLVM represents as a constant-sized copy — no heap allocation and no slice indirection survives the optimizer. The loop reduces to a 4-byte store per iteration and is SIMD-vectorizable. The finder's concern about 'slice indirection' describes source semantics, not emitted code.

title: decoder.info() called twice — once after read_info and once after decode — when the second call is only needed for pixel_format
description: Lines 45-47 call `decoder.info()` to obtain width/height before decoding; lines 63-65 call it again post-decode solely to get `pixel_format`. This is harmless in terms of correctness but creates an extra struct-copy per decode call. The pixel_format could be captured alongside the first `info()` call since the JPEG color space does not change between `read_info` and `decode`. Consolidating to one `info()` call eliminates a redundant struct copy.
evidence: Verified against jpeg-decoder-0.3.2 source: `ImageInfo.pixel_format` is derived from `frame.components.len()` and `frame.precision` at `read_info` time and is unchanged by `decode`. The first `info()` call already captures `pixel_format`; the second call at lines 63-65 is used only for `decoded.pixel_format` and is provably redundant. The fix is one line: use `info.pixel_format` instead of calling `info()` again.

title: No WASM timing instrumentation — decode latency per scale factor is invisible to callers
description: The `decode_scaled` function has no timing hooks. Since this is a WASM-exported entry point called from JavaScript, callers have no way to distinguish DCT-decode time from pixel-conversion time without browser profiler sampling. Adding optional `performance.now()` timestamps returned in `DecodeResult` (or a separate `DecodeStats` struct) would allow the JS layer to surface per-call latency to the scheduler's budget accounting, consistent with the rest of the pipeline's EMA-based drain tracking.
code_excerpt:
#[wasm_bindgen]
pub fn decode_scaled(jpeg: &[u8], denom: u8) -> Result<DecodeResult, JsValue> {
evidence: Whether timing hooks are needed depends on whether `fast-jpeg` is wired into the scheduler's budget accounting. The benchmark scripts (`timings/fastest/`) already capture `performance.now()` externally around `decode_scaled` calls. It is unclear from this section alone whether the scheduler's EMA drain tracking needs per-call timing from the WASM boundary itself, or whether external measurement suffices.
